worth_it_explanation: Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) produces a full demographic breakdown of who a campaign reached by age and gender, but that breakdown is only useful if someone reads it against the intended target. These checks confirm that the delivered composition is compared to the demographic goal, that the age and gender data is read at a level of detail that reveals where off-target delivery went rather than only whether the on-target percentage passed a threshold, and that composition is reviewed by publisher or placement so a single skewing source does not hide inside the campaign average. None of these require additional vendor spend; they require applying the data CCR already produces.
intro: Comscore Campaign Ratings measures the demographic composition of a campaign's audience, reporting the age and gender breakdown of who the campaign reached across desktop, mobile, and connected TV (CTV). The core output includes on-target percentage, unique reach counts by demographic cell, Gross Rating Points (GRPs), average frequency, and audience reach as a share of each demographic's population. These checks focus on whether that composition data is actually used to understand and improve campaign performance, not just collected and filed.

audit_checks.title: Delivered demographic composition is not compared to the intended target demographic after the campaign runs
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A campaign brief defines a target demographic, such as adults 25-54 or women 18-34, and the buy is structured to reach that group. If the delivered composition from the CCR report is never compared to that target, there is no feedback loop between what was planned and what was delivered. The next campaign repeats the same placements and targeting without knowing whether the prior flight hit or missed the intended audience.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) produces a demographic profile of who a campaign actually reached. The report shows unique reach counts, on-target percentage (OTP), and audience share broken out by age band and gender. That profile describes the delivered audience, not the intended one. The delivered and intended audiences are often not the same.

On-target percentage measures the share of impressions that landed within the specified demographic target. A campaign targeting adults 25-54 with an OTP of 62% delivered 38% of its impressions outside that range. CCR reports where those off-target impressions went by age band and gender cell, but that data is only useful if the report is compared to the campaign brief's demographic specification.

Without that comparison, the post-campaign read is missing its baseline. A 62% OTP looks different depending on whether the goal was 65%, 75%, or 55%. The age and gender cells that the off-target impressions landed in tell the team whether the skew is directional (for example, consistently reaching an older audience than intended) or scattered. Directional skew points to a targeting configuration or supply mix that is pulling in the wrong direction and can be corrected on the next flight.

CCR uses a multi-sourced methodology that combines census-calibrated panels with deterministic signals to report reach and frequency at the demographic cell level. The report exists for this purpose, but the comparison to the campaign brief has to be performed by the team reviewing the data.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The post-campaign or end-of-flight report includes the CCR on-target percentage but does not show the intended demographic goal alongside it for comparison
  • The team can report the OTP but cannot describe which specific age or gender cells received the off-target impressions
  • The campaign brief exists but is not referenced during the post-campaign measurement review
You may want to confirm that whoever reviews the CCR report has access to the original campaign brief and uses the target demographic specification as the benchmark when reading the delivered composition.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer targeting a specific demographic for a seasonal campaign, such as women 25-44 ahead of a product launch, the delivered composition tells you whether that audience was actually reached or whether spend leaked into other groups. You may want to confirm the post-campaign review compares the CCR demographic breakdown to the brief's target specification before making creative or targeting decisions for the next flight.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser buying against a defined professional or consumer demographic, a CCR report that shows a significant share of impressions reached an age group or gender outside the target is a signal that the supply mix or targeting parameters need adjustment. You may want to confirm the delivered demographic composition is reviewed against the campaign's stated audience goal, not evaluated in isolation.

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore Campaign Ratings methodology documentation, including the full explanation of how on-target percentage is calculated and how demographic cells are defined, is available to Comscore clients through the CCR portal and via your Comscore account team. Public documentation on industry-standard demographic measurement practices is available from the Media Rating Council.

investigate.intro: Locate the CCR report for the campaign. Confirm whether the on-target percentage and demographic cell breakdown are reviewed alongside the original campaign target demographic.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Open the CCR report for the campaign. Locate the on-target percentage column, which shows what share of impressions landed within the specified demographic.
Find the demographic breakdown rows. These show unique reach counts and impression share by age band and gender cell (e.g., M18-24, F25-34, M35-49).
Compare the cells with the highest impression share or unique reach counts to the campaign brief's stated target demographic. Note whether the highest-volume cells match the target.
Ask the team whether this comparison was documented in the post-campaign or end-of-flight review. If no documentation exists, the comparison may not have been performed.

Record the OTP, the target demographic as stated in the brief, and the top two non-target cells by impression volume. This combination shows the size of the miss and where the missed impressions went.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the campaign's CCR on-target percentage, the intended target demographic from the campaign brief, and whether a documented comparison between the two exists in the post-campaign review. A campaign with an OTP within 5 percentage points of the stated goal and a documented comparison passes. A campaign where no comparison to the intended target was performed, or where the OTP falls materially below the goal with no documented explanation, is a fail, recorded with the OTP found and the cells that received the highest share of off-target impressions.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign's on-target percentage, the intended target demographic from the brief, and whether a post-campaign comparison was documented above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Comscore Campaign Ratings on-target percentage for campaigns on {{site_name}} is reviewed as a standalone metric without comparison to the intended target demographic from the campaign brief. A delivered composition that is not measured against the stated goal provides no actionable feedback for the next flight. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a post-campaign review step that places the CCR demographic breakdown alongside the brief's target specification, noting the on-target percentage relative to the goal and identifying the age and gender cells that received the highest share of off-target impressions.

audit_checks.title: Demographic composition is evaluated only as an on-target percentage pass or fail rather than by the full age and gender breakdown
audit_checks.impact_rationale: On-target percentage collapses the full demographic composition into a single number. A campaign that reaches a broad target such as adults 18-49 may hit a passing OTP while the on-target impressions concentrated in one corner of that range, such as adults 45-49. The team sees a pass but cannot tell where within the target the delivery actually landed or which age cells outside the target absorbed the off-target impressions.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) reports demographic composition at the individual cell level. Each row in the audience breakdown corresponds to a specific age band and gender combination, such as males 18-24, females 35-49, or adults 25-34. The report provides unique reach count, on-target impressions, impression share, audience reach percentage, Gross Rating Points (GRPs), and average frequency for each cell.

On-target percentage is calculated by dividing the impressions served to the specified target demographic by the total impression count. That calculation treats the target demographic as a single group. If the target is adults 25-54, any impression reaching someone in that range counts as on-target regardless of whether it reached a 26-year-old or a 53-year-old, a male or a female.

The cell-level data reveals patterns that OTP hides. A campaign targeting women 25-44 may report a 70% OTP while the audience breakdown shows that the majority of on-target impressions reached women 35-44 and very few reached women 25-34. If the product is aimed at younger women in that range, the composition skew matters even though the OTP looks acceptable. The cell-level data also shows which off-target groups absorbed the remaining 30% of impressions, information that is needed to adjust supply sourcing or targeting on the next flight.

CCR produces reach and GRP breakdowns by demographic cell that are designed for this analysis. Reviewing only the summary OTP leaves this detail unread and the composition picture incomplete.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The post-campaign review reports only the single OTP figure and does not include a breakdown of which demographic cells received on-target and off-target impressions
  • The team can confirm whether the campaign passed its OTP goal but cannot describe which age bands drove the on-target number or where the off-target impressions went
  • The CCR report was accessed but only the summary row was included in the post-campaign document
You may want to confirm that the full demographic cell breakdown, not just the summary OTP, is reviewed before making supply or targeting decisions based on demographic performance.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer targeting a broad demographic such as adults 18-49, a passing OTP does not confirm that the intended age group within that range received the majority of impressions. You may want to review the CCR audience breakdown at the cell level to confirm that the product's core buyer demographic, not just any cell within the broad target range, received meaningful reach.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser with a specific buyer persona within a broad target demographic, the cell-level CCR breakdown reveals whether the intended persona age and gender combination received proportionate delivery or whether another cell within the target absorbed most of the on-target impressions. You may want to confirm the audience breakdown is reviewed at this level of detail before reporting demographic performance to the client.

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore Campaign Ratings methodology documentation, including how demographic cells are defined and how GRPs and reach are calculated per cell, is available to Comscore clients through the CCR portal and via your Comscore account team. The Media Rating Council's Digital Audience-Based Measurement Standards describe the industry-standard framework for demographic cell-level audience reporting.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the post-campaign demographic review includes the full CCR audience breakdown by age band and gender cell, or only the summary on-target percentage.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Open the CCR report for the campaign. Confirm whether the report is configured to show results grouped by demographic cell (age band and gender) rather than showing only a summary OTP row.
Review the impression share and GRP columns for each demographic cell. Note cells with a disproportionate share of total impressions relative to their share of the overall target.
Identify the top three cells by unique reach count. Confirm whether these cells all fall within the intended target demographic or whether one or more high-volume cells fall outside the target.
Ask the team whether this cell-level view was included in the post-campaign review or whether only the summary OTP was reported.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the post-campaign demographic review included the full CCR audience breakdown at the cell level or only the summary OTP. A review that includes the cell-level breakdown, identifies the top delivery cells relative to the intended target, and notes any over-indexed non-target cells passes. A review that reported only the summary OTP without cell-level detail is a fail, recorded with the top three cells by unique reach count and whether each falls within or outside the campaign's target demographic.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the post-campaign review included the CCR demographic breakdown at the cell level and list the top three cells by unique reach count above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s post-campaign demographic reviews report the Comscore Campaign Ratings on-target percentage as a summary figure without examining the full age and gender breakdown. On-target percentage collapses all cells within the target demographic into a single number, which can obscure significant concentration in one corner of the target range and does not identify which non-target cells received off-target impressions. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the CCR audience breakdown at the cell level after each flight, noting the cells with the highest impression share and GRPs within and outside the target, and including that breakdown in post-campaign documentation so targeting and supply decisions on subsequent flights are grounded in the actual delivered composition.

audit_checks.title: Demographic composition from CCR is reviewed only at the campaign level, not broken out by publisher or placement
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Campaign-level demographic composition aggregates across every publisher and placement in the buy. If one publisher or placement delivers a demographic profile that skews significantly away from the campaign target, that skew is diluted by other sources that perform closer to the goal. The aggregate looks acceptable while a specific source is systematically reaching the wrong audience.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) can break down demographic composition by publisher or placement when the measurement setup and reporting configuration support it. The CCR report includes a site or publisher dimension that allows the delivered demographic breakdown to be viewed separately for each source in the campaign rather than only as a campaign-wide aggregate.

Campaign-level aggregation combines the demographic output of every publisher in the buy into a single profile. Two publishers that each deliver 50% of the campaign's impressions could produce very different demographic compositions. If one skews significantly older than the target and the other delivers exactly on target, the campaign-level profile will appear to be a moderate miss, but the actual picture is that one source is performing correctly and one is not. Without the per-publisher breakdown, the team cannot identify which source is responsible for the skew.

This distinction matters for the decisions that follow a campaign. If the team concludes that the demographic miss was campaign-wide and reduces the overall budget or changes all creatives in response, a source that was performing correctly will also be affected. If the team identifies the specific publisher or placement responsible for the skew, the correction can be targeted: renegotiate with that publisher, shift budget away from placements where the age and gender profile diverges from the campaign target, or request demographic transparency from that source before the next buy.

Not all CCR implementations produce publisher-level demographic data. Availability depends on how the measurement tags are deployed and how the CCR report is configured. When publisher-level demographic data is not available, the team should note this as a limitation of the current measurement setup rather than assuming the campaign-level aggregate is representative of all sources.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The CCR report reviewed after the campaign shows composition data only for the full campaign with no breakdown by publisher, placement, or site
  • The campaign ran across a mix of publishers or supply types (open exchange, private marketplace, and direct inventory) but all are reported together in the demographic composition output
  • A demographic skew was noted at the campaign level but the team could not determine which publisher or placement drove it
You may want to confirm whether the platform's CCR implementation supports a publisher or site breakdown and, if so, that this view is included in the standard post-campaign demographic review.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a campaign across multiple publishers or supply types, a demographic miss at the campaign level may trace back to one specific publisher whose audience composition differs from the others. You may want to confirm the CCR report is reviewed at the publisher or placement level, not only at the campaign aggregate, so budget decisions between sources are based on demographic performance rather than campaign-level averages.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser buying across open exchange and direct or private marketplace (PMP) inventory, the demographic composition of each supply type can differ significantly. You may want to confirm that the CCR composition data is broken out by supply source so the team can identify whether the open exchange or the direct placements are responsible for off-target demographic delivery.

educate.learn_more_note: Publisher-level and placement-level demographic reporting in Comscore Campaign Ratings depends on how measurement tags are deployed and how the CCR report is configured. Documentation on available report dimensions and setup requirements is available to Comscore clients through the CCR portal and via your Comscore account team.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the CCR report for the campaign includes a publisher or placement breakdown of the demographic composition, and whether this view was reviewed post-campaign.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Open the CCR report for the campaign. Check whether the report is configured to include a site, publisher, or placement dimension, or whether results are shown only for the full campaign.
If a publisher or site breakdown is available, pull the demographic composition segmented by source. Compare the on-target percentage and top demographic cells across publishers.

Identify any publisher or placement where the on-target percentage falls more than 10 percentage points below the campaign average or where a non-target age and gender cell accounts for a disproportionate share of that source's impressions.

If no publisher- or placement-level breakdown is available in the current report setup, note this as a measurement gap. Ask the team whether this level of detail can be enabled for future campaigns given the CCR implementation in use.
Record which publisher or placement, if any, shows the most significant demographic skew relative to the campaign average.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the post-campaign CCR review included a publisher or placement breakdown of the demographic composition, or only a campaign-level aggregate. A review that includes per-publisher or per-placement demographic data, identifies any source with a skew greater than 10 percentage points from the campaign average, and documents the finding passes. A review that relied only on the campaign-level aggregate, with no publisher breakdown available or reviewed, is a fail, recorded with the campaign-level OTP and a note on whether publisher-level data was available but not used or was unavailable due to the measurement configuration.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the CCR report included a publisher or placement breakdown and note the campaign-level on-target percentage above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Comscore Campaign Ratings demographic composition reviews aggregate all publishers and placements into a single campaign-level profile without a breakdown by source. When a demographic skew is present at the campaign level, an aggregate view does not identify which publisher or placement is responsible, so budget and targeting corrections cannot be directed at the specific source causing the miss. {{voice_recommend}} pulling the CCR publisher or site breakdown after each campaign to compare the demographic composition across sources, flagging any publisher or placement where the on-target percentage or age and gender cell distribution differs significantly from the campaign average, and incorporating that per-source view into post-campaign reporting and the next-flight planning process.
